13: Peter's 'submit to every human institution' (hypotassō pase anthropine ktisei) parallels Paul's Romans 13, but the social context is different: 1 Peter addresses communities facing active hostility and false accusation, not merely civic obligation. The political submission is part of the apologetic strategy: by visibly good behavior (1 Peter 2:15: agathopoia, doing good), the community silences the ignorance of foolish critics. The 'honor everyone, love the brotherhood, fear God, honor the emperor' (1 Peter 2:17) places the emperor in the fourth position — honored, but not uniquely so, and subordinate to God-fear.
1: 'Therefore, rid yourselves of all malice and all deceit, hypocrisy, envy, and slander of every kind' — the 'therefore' (oun) connects the ethical imperative to the new birth through the living word (1 Peter 1:23-25). The vice list (malice, deceit, hypocrisy, envy, slander) describes the social pathologies of competitive Greco-Roman urban culture: the zero-sum world of honor and shame in which advancement required putting others down. Peter's communities, formed by the gospel-word, are called to a different social practice. The 'putting away' (apothemenoi) is a clothing metaphor — stripping off the old character garments.
2: 'Like newborn babies, crave pure spiritual milk, so that by it you may grow up in your salvation' — the nursing metaphor for instruction was widespread in Hellenistic educational culture: Philo, Epictetus, and others used the milk-to-solid-food progression for intellectual development. Peter adapts it for the new birth context (1 Peter 1:23): those just born again need the nourishment appropriate to their new life. The 'pure spiritual milk' (to logikon adolon gala) — literally 'rational/word milk without guile' — may echo the Stoic logos concept while pointing to the word of the gospel.
3: 'Now that you have tasted that the Lord is good' — a direct citation of Psalms 34:8 LXX (geusasthe kai idete hoti chrēstos ho kyrios: taste and see that the Lord is good/gracious). The wordplay on chrēstos (good, gracious) and Christos (Christ, the Anointed) — homophone in Greek — made Psalms 34:8 an especially apt text for early Christians: the goodness tasted is the goodness of Christ. The community's conversion experience is described as a 'tasting' — direct sensory encounter with the character of the Lord.
4: 'As you come to him, the living Stone — rejected by humans but chosen by God and precious to him' — the 'living stone' (lithon zōnta) conflates the stone imagery from the OT stone-passages (Isaiah 28:16; Psalms 118:22; Isaiah 8:14) with the resurrection: the stone that was rejected became the living stone. In Jewish tradition, the 'stone' that the builders rejected (Psalms 118:22) was variously interpreted as Abraham, David, or the Messiah. The 'coming to' him (proserchomenoi) is the approach to the sanctuary — pilgrimage and worship language — applied to the community's ongoing relationship with the risen Lord.
5: 'You also, like living stones, are being built into a spiritual house to be a holy priesthood, offering spiritual sacrifices acceptable to God through Jesus Christ' — the 'spiritual house' (oikos pneumatikos) and 'holy priesthood' (hierateuma hagion) democratize the Levitical and temple institutions. The naos (inner sanctuary) was accessible only to priests; the outer courts to laypeople. Peter's community is being built as the sanctuary itself, with every member serving as priest. The 'spiritual sacrifices' (pneumatikas thysias) are the community's worship — prayer, praise, moral life — offered through the mediating role of Christ.
6: 'See, I lay a stone in Zion, a chosen and precious cornerstone, and the one who trusts in him will never be put to shame' — Peter quotes Isaiah 28:16 LXX, a text that in its original context addressed the Assyrian crisis: God's foundation-stone in Zion (perhaps the Davidic king) was more reliable than any foreign alliance. The LXX adds 'the one who trusts' (ho pisteuōn) — missing from the MT — making explicit the faith-element that Peter foregrounds. The stone in Zion becomes in Peter's exegesis the crucified and risen Christ who is laid as the community's foundation.
7: 'Now to you who believe, this stone is precious. But to those who do not believe, 'The stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone'' — Peter quotes Psalms 118:22, a pilgrimage psalm sung at festival processions entering Jerusalem. Its 'rejected stone' was read in early Christian tradition as the crucifixion and resurrection: Jesus rejected by the temple establishment became the cornerstone (Mark 12:10-11; Acts 4:11). The contrast between believers ('precious to you') and non-believers ('a stone they stumble over') mirrors the double effect of the gospel in Paul (1 Corinthians 1:18: the word of the cross is folly to those perishing, but power to those being saved).
8: 'A stone that causes people to stumble and a rock that makes them fall. They stumble because they disobey the message' — Peter adds Isaiah 8:14 (the LORD will become 'a stone that causes people to stumble and a rock that makes them fall for both houses of Israel'). In Isaiah 8, the stone of stumbling was the LORD himself, whose word of judgment the unfaithful rejected. The application to Christ who is both stumbling block and cornerstone maintains the identification of Jesus with the LORD of Isaiah — the same divine Person who was both the sanctuary and the scandal in Isaiah's day.
9: 'But you are a chosen people, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, God's special possession, that you may declare the praises of him who called you out of darkness into his wonderful light' — the fourfold identity-declaration applies Exodus 19:6 (kingdom of priests, holy nation) and Isaiah 43:20-21 (my chosen people, the people I formed for myself that they may proclaim my praise) to Gentile Christians. The community that has no ethnic heritage in Israel, no ancestral claim on the land, and no civic standing in the Roman empire is given the highest possible covenantal identity: the identity that was Israel's at Sinai.
10: 'Once you were not a people, but now you are the people of God; once you had not received mercy, but now you have received mercy' — Peter cites Hosea 1:6, 9 (Lo-Ammi: not my people; Lo-Ruhamah: not loved/not received mercy) and Hosea 2:23 (I will call them 'my people' who are not my people). Hosea addressed the northern kingdom of Israel that had broken covenant with God; Peter applies the restoration-language to Gentile Christians who stand in the position of 'not a people' before their conversion. The mercy that restores 'not my people' to 'my people' is realized in the gospel.
11: 'Dear friends, I urge you, as foreigners and exiles, to abstain from sinful desires, which wage war against your soul' — the paroikous kai parepidēmous (resident aliens and temporary visitors) returns to the social-status terminology of 1 Peter 1:1, 17. A paroikos was a free person who resided in a city without citizenship — paying taxes, subject to local law, but without civic rights or political voice. A parepidēmos was an even more temporary visitor. Peter's ethical argument: their precarious social status, rather than being a disadvantage, is a clarifying context for Christian ethics — the soul's warfare against sinful desire is more visible when you do not belong to the city.
12: 'Live such good lives among the pagans that, though they accuse you of doing wrong, they may see your good deeds and glorify God on the day he visits us' — the 'day of visitation' (en hēmera episkopēs) is the eschatological coming of God, an OT image (Isaiah 10:3; Jeremiah 6:15; Wis 1 Peter 3:7) in which God's coming brings both judgment and vindication. The strategy of visible good conduct in the face of false accusations is the community's public apologetic: not argument but life. The accusation of wrongdoing was a real feature of Roman-era hostility to Christians — Tacitus and Pliny both reference accusations of 'hatred of the human race.'
14: 'Or to governors, who are sent by him to punish those who do wrong and to commend those who do right' — the provincial governors (hēgemones) were the emperor's representatives exercising judicial and administrative power in the provinces where Peter's communities lived (Pliny the Younger served as governor of Bithynia-Pontus c. 111-113 CE, decades after this letter; but his correspondence with Trajan about Christians shows the pattern Peter describes). The submission to governors is grounded in their God-ordained function of punishing wrongdoing and commending good — a function that Christian good conduct can engage positively.
15: 'For it is God's will that by doing good you should silence the ignorant talk of foolish people' — the 'silencing' (phimoun, to muzzle) of ignorant accusations through good conduct is a public-apologetic strategy. The accusations Peter's communities face — atheism (refusing to honor the gods), misanthropy (social withdrawal from civic religious events), possibly even cannibalism and sexual immorality (misunderstandings of the Lord's Supper and 'love feasts') — are countered not primarily by reasoned defense but by visible conduct that contradicts the accusations.
16: 'Live as free people, but do not use your freedom as a cover-up for evil; live as God's slaves' — the paradox of free-slaves (eleutheroi... douloi theou) captures the Christian freedom-within-obligation structure. In the Greco-Roman world, freedom (eleutheria) and slavery (douleia) were absolute opposites in social terms; the only exception was the philosopher's claim that the truly free person is the one who has conquered their passions, while the apparently free person enslaved to passion is truly a slave. Peter adapts this: genuine freedom is the freedom of those who are God's slaves — not subject to human compulsion while fully submitted to the divine will.
17: 'Show proper respect to everyone, love the family of believers, fear God, honor the emperor' — the four-part summary moves from universal respect (panta timēsate, honor everyone) to the particular love of the brotherhood to the fear of God to the honor of the emperor. The placement of the emperor after 'fear God' is deliberate: the emperor is to be honored (timēsate) but God alone is to be feared (phobeisthe) — a subtle distinction that relativizes imperial authority within the larger framework of divine sovereignty.
18: 'Slaves, in reverent fear of God submit yourselves to your masters, not only to those who are good and considerate, but also to those who are harsh' — the household code for enslaved persons (oiketai, house-slaves, specifically) was a feature of Hellenistic ethical teaching. Peter's code differs from its Greco-Roman parallels in grounding submission not in social order or master-benefit but in 'reverent fear of God' (en phobo theou) — the enslaved person's conduct is an act of worship, not social compliance. The asymmetric command (submit even to harsh masters) prepares for the Christ-suffering pattern that follows in 1 Peter 2:21-25.
19: 'For it is commendable if someone bears up under the pain of unjust suffering because they are conscious of God' — the key phrase is 'conscious of God' (dia syneidesin theou) — awareness of God's reality and claim. Stoic philosophy commended the bearing of unjust suffering as evidence of virtue and inner freedom; Peter transforms this: the grounding is not self-mastery but God-consciousness. The person who suffers unjustly while remaining 'conscious of God' — aware that the divine eye sees and the divine Judge is coming — is commended not for stoic endurance but for theocentric faithfulness.
20: 'But if you suffer for doing good and you endure it, this is commendable before God' — Peter intensifies the argument: not only is God-conscious endurance of unjust suffering praiseworthy, it is specifically praiseworthy in God's estimation. The 'commendation before God' (charis para theō) is what the Hellenistic system called charis — the patron's favorable response to a client's good conduct. Peter reframes the honor-shame economy entirely: the judgment that matters is not the master's, not the city's, not the emperor's, but God's alone. The enslaved person who endures unjust suffering has God as the patron whose honor counts.
21: 'To this you were called, because Christ suffered for you, leaving you an example, that you should follow in his steps' — the calling of enslaved persons to suffer unjustly is grounded in Christ's own suffering 'for you' (hyper hymōn) — substitutionary in nature, yet also exemplary. The word 'example' (hypogrammon) was a writing-practice term: the tracing-copy that students followed line-by-line to learn penmanship; Christ's suffering is the pattern to trace, not merely to admire from a distance. The 'following in his steps' (epakolouthēsēte tois ichnesin autou) is discipleship language: the same footprints that Christ left, believers are called to walk in.
22: 'He committed no sin, and no deceit was found in his mouth' — Peter quotes Isaiah 53:9 almost verbatim. The 'Servant' of Isaiah 53 who 'had done no violence, and there was no deceit in his mouth' is identified with Jesus Christ, whose sinless death was the qualification for his atoning role. That Peter cites Isa 53 in the context of an instruction to enslaved persons is significant: the Servant who suffered unjustly at the hands of oppressive power is precisely the enslaved person's solidarity figure — the one who experienced the worst of what power can do to the powerless.
23: 'When they hurled their insults at him, he did not retaliate; when he suffered, he made no threats. Instead, he entrusted himself to him who judges justly' — the non-retaliation is not passivity but the active surrender of the right to vindication to the one who will judge rightly. The 'entrusting' (paredidou de tō krinonti dikaiōs) is the handing-over of one's cause to the divine judge — the posture of Psalms 31:5 (into your hands I commit my spirit) and of Jesus's own cry from the cross (Luke 23:46). For enslaved persons in the first-century context, this is not a counsel of permanent submission to injustice but a posture of eschatological confidence in the one whose judgment will ultimately prevail.
24: 'He himself bore our sins in his body on the cross, so that we might die to sins and live for righteousness; by his wounds you have been healed' — Peter continues the Isa 53 catena: Isaiah 53:4-5 (surely he took up our infirmities... he was pierced for our transgressions... by his wounds we are healed) and 1 Peter 53:12 (he bore the sin of many). The 'cross' is literally 'the wood' (xylou, the tree) — Acts 5:30; 10:39 use the same term for the cross, which also evokes Deuteronomy 21:22-23 (anyone hung on a tree is under God's curse) — the curse-bearing dimension of Christ's death that Paul develops in Galatians 3:13.
25: 'For you were like sheep going astray, but now you have returned to the Shepherd and Overseer of your souls' — the shepherd-sheep imagery from Isaiah 53:6 (all we like sheep have gone astray; each has turned to his own way) is paired with the 'Shepherd and Overseer' (ton poimena kai episkopon) titles for Christ. Episkopos (overseer, bishop) was a management term in Greco-Roman administration (overseers of building projects, financial overseers); in the LXX it translates the Hebrew paqid (appointed officer). Peter applies the double title to Christ as the supreme Shepherd-Overseer under whom the community's human elders (1 Peter 5:1-4) exercise delegated authority.
